coredata.dc:title: pH-responsive and targeted delivery of rutin for breast cancer therapy via folic acid-functionalized carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Tejwan, Neeraj
Sadhukhan, Pritam
Sharma, Anirudh
Singh, Th. Abhishek
Hatimuria, Madushmita
Pabbathi, Ashok
Das, Joydeep
Sil, Parames C.
coredata.dc:description: Here, first we have synthesized carbon dots (CDs) from red Korean ginseng, and modified further with folic acid (FA) for the targeted and controlled delivery of a natual flavonoid, rutin (RUT). FA conjugated and morin loaded CDs (FA-CDs-RUT) displayed size ~70 nm, drug loading content of 14.5 % and a pH-dependent drug release behaviour. Human breast cancer MCF-7 cells were taken for checking the anticancer activities of FA-CDs-RUT. The nanohybrids (FA-CDs-RUT) showed a dose-dependent (10–50 μg/mL) cytotoxicity in MCF-7 cells and caused apoptotic cell death by inducing oxidative stress and mitochondrial damage. Besides, anti-migratory role of FA-CDs-RUT was also observed. The in vivo intravenous (i.v.) administration of FA-CDs-RUT nanohybrid was found to effectively decrease the tumor growth in tumor-bearing mice as compared with bare rutin and CDs. Interestingly, none of the nanoformulations and bare rutin caused any significant alterations in blood biochemical parameters. The enhanced anticancer activity of FA-CDs-RUT is thought to be due to combinatorial anticancer effects of CDs and rutin. We also performed docking study of rutin with Bcl-2 to gain insights into possible pathways of cell growth regulation. Molecular docking study shown that rutin binds with Bcl-2 with binding affinity of −7.6 k Cal/mol. Together, this study provides insight into the usability of FA-CDs-RUT to treat breast cancer with their mechanisms of action.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Breast cancer
Carbon dots
Drug delivery
Rutin
pH-responsive
Reactive oxygen species

Cytotoxicity and reactive oxygen species (ROS) measurement in MCF-7 cells
Development of in vivo solid tumor model and anticancer activity checking
In vitro anticancer activity of FA-CDs-RUT nanoparticles
Oxidative stress and induction of apoptosis
In vitro effect of FA-CDs-RUT on retardation of cell migration
In vivo anticancer activity and systemic toxicity assessment of FA-CDs-RUT nanoparticles

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of FA-CDs-RUT; (b) DLS data of CDs & FA-CDs-RUT; and (c) FTIR spectra of CDs, RUT& FA-CDs-RUT.
(a) UV Spectra of CDs, RUT& FA-CDs-RUT; and (b) pH and time dependent RUT release from FA-CDs-RUT. All values are expressed as mean ± SD.
(a) Cell viability; (b) Cellular morphology; and (c) Intracellular ROS levels in MCF-7 cells. All values are expressed as mean ± SD.
(a) Western blot analysis; and (b) Wound healing assay using MCF-7 cells.
Transwell migration assay using MCF-7 cells.
(a) & (b) Change in tumor volume and tumor weight; (c) Photographs of tumor removed from different groups; (d) Change in body weight curve. All values are expressed as mean ± SD.
Serum biochemistry analysis related to hepatic and renal toxicity. All values are expressed as mean ± SD.
Binding pose of Rutin with Bcl-2 (a) and 2D interaction plot (b).
Drug loading content (DLC) and Drug encapsulation efficiency (DEE).

head.ce:title: pH-responsive and targeted delivery of rutin for breast cancer therapy via folic acid-functionalized carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, School of Physical Sciences, Mizoram University, Aizawl 796004, Mizoram, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, School of Physical Sciences, Mizoram University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Physical Sciences, Mizoram University, Aizawl-796004, Mizoram, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here, first we have synthesized carbon dots (CDs) from red Korean ginseng, and modified further with folic acid (FA) for the targeted and controlled delivery of a natual flavonoid, rutin (RUT). FA conjugated and morin loaded CDs (FA-CDs-RUT) displayed size ~70 nm, drug loading content of 14.5 % and a pH-dependent drug release behaviour. Human breast cancer MCF-7 cells were taken for checking the anticancer activities of FA-CDs-RUT. The nanohybrids (FA-CDs-RUT) showed a dose-dependent (10–50 μg/mL) cytotoxicity in MCF-7 cells and caused apoptotic cell death by inducing oxidative stress and mitochondrial damage. Besides, anti-migratory role of FA-CDs-RUT was also observed. The in vivo intravenous (i.v.) administration of FA-CDs-RUT nanohybrid was found to effectively decrease the tumor growth in tumor-bearing mice as compared with bare rutin and CDs. Interestingly, none of the nanoformulations and bare rutin caused any significant alterations in blood biochemical parameters. The enhanced anticancer activity of FA-CDs-RUT is thought to be due to combinatorial anticancer effects of CDs and rutin. We also performed docking study of rutin with Bcl-2 to gain insights into possible pathways of cell growth regulation. Molecular docking study shown that rutin binds with Bcl-2 with binding affinity of −7.6 k Cal/mol. Together, this study provides insight into the usability of FA-CDs-RUT to treat breast cancer with their mechanisms of action.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer is recognized as one of the major cause of morbidity and mortality every year. It is caused due to the abnormal cell division and heterogeneous cell growth, which finally form a malignant neoplasm [1,2]. About 18 million new cases registered every year. At present, targeted drug delivery is an emerging approach for the treatment of cancer due to their high efficiency and low biodegradability of the drug. Various nanoparticle-based drug carriers like carbon dots (CDs), metal and non-metal nanoparticles (NPs), solid lipids, liposomes, dendrimers, magnetic nanoparticles, polymers and micelles have been used for this purpose [3–8]. Among them, CDs are the newly discovered member with size less than 10 nm [9]. They can be prepared from various chemical and natural precursors via top down and bottom up approaches [10,11]. Nowadays, researchers are focusing on several plant derived CDs with significant biomedical applications.

Plants are known to be enriched with several natural compounds with significant medicinal properties [12]. Numerous diseases can be prevented and cured by using herbal medicines and thereby considered as the traditional methods of remedy [13]. Ginseng is one of the mostly used herb with several therapeutic purposes such as antiapoptotic, antiinflammatory, antitumor, antidiabetic, and antistress agent [14]. Ginsenosides are considered as the main component of ginseng. Therefore, in our present study we used red Korean ginseng root extracts for the synthesis of CDs and used them for targeted drug delivery into breast cancer cells. Various hydrophobic biomolecules can be conjugated via electrostatic interactions or π-π* stacking on CD surface [15].

The present study focusses on the synthesis of folic acid (FA) functionalized CDs from red Korean Ginseng using microwave irradiation method followed by conjugation with a natural bioflavonoid, rutin (RUT) via π-π* stacking. Rutin, a bioflavonoid present in a number of fruits and vegetables is well known for its significant antibacterial, cardioprotective, vasoprotective, antioxidant, cytoprotective, anticarcinogenic and neuroprotective activities [16]. The main objective of the study was to check the anticancer activity of RUT after its targeted delivery via CDs in folic acid overexpressed human MCF-7 breast cancer cell lines.

Folic acid (FA), N- hydroxyl succinimide, 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethyl carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC∙HCl), and rutin were purchased from Loba Chemie Pvt. Ltd. (Maharashtra, India). Disodium dihydrogen phosphate dihydrate, potassium dihydrogen phosphate, and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were obtained from Central Drug House Pvt. Ltd. (New Delhi, India). 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT) was obtained from “Merck” Ltd. (Mumbai, Maharashtra). The media, antibiotics and serum required for animal cell culture were procured from Hi-Media (Mumbai, India) and Thermo Scientific Hy-Clone (Logan, Utah, USA). Red Korean ginseng (RKG) used to prepare CDs was obtained online from amazon.in. Millipore system purified deionized water of conductivity 18 Ω cm−1 has been used in this study. All the chemicals were used without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of folic acid conjugated carbon dots (CDs) and their characterization
ce:section.ce:para: The detailed synthesis procedure of CDs from red Korean Ginseng via microwave irradiation and their characterization techniques were described in our earlier publication [17,18]. Briefly, the Ginseng extract was microwaved using a domestic microoven (power: 700 W, frequency: 2450 MHz) for seven consecutive cycles (2 mins each) and cooled for 2 mins after every cycle. Finally the brown mass was dissolved in water, dialysed and freeze dried to obtain pure CD powder. For the preparation of folic acid conjugated CDs, the carboxylic acid groups on CD surface were activated by adding an equivalent amount(120 mg) of EHS and NHS for 560 mg of CDs. The reaction mixture was stirred for 3 h. Then, 600 mg FA was dissolved in DMSO and mixed with activated CDs. Thereafter, the solution was stirred for 24 h to achieve FA-CDs NPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Rutin (RUT)/drug loading to CDs and release behaviour
ce:section.ce:para: The detailed procedures for drug loading, determination of drug loading content (DLC) and drug release behaviour via UV–Vis spectroscopy were described in our earlier publication [17].

In brief, RUT was loaded on CD surface in a 1:1 mass ratio via simply mixing aqueous solution of CDs with rutin in DMSO for overnight under stirring condition at room temperature. Finally the solution was dialysed and freeze dried to obtain pure drug-load CD powder.

The optical absorbance at 340 nm was measured using an UV–Vis spectrophotometerbefore as well as after RUT loading to check the drug loading content (DLC) and drug encapsulation efficiency (DEE). DLC was calculated using a linear regression equation, y = 0.3289x + 0.6411 (R2 = 0.98) obtained from the calibration curve of RUT solutions with known concentrations.

The DLC as well as DEE have been quantified by using the following formulae:
DLC
%
=
Weight of rutin in
NPs
/
Weight of
NPs
× 100 %
DEE
%
=
Weight of rutin in
NPs
/
Weight of rutin taken
× 100 %

Here we checked pH-dependent release behaviour of RUT in different buffer solutions (pH 5.5 and 7.4) by using dialysis diffusion technique.The amount of drug (rutin) released into the release media was quantified following 6, 12, 24, and 48 h via checking the optical absorbance values of rutin at 340 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity and reactive oxygen species (ROS) measurement in MCF-7 cells
ce:section.ce:para: Human breast adenocarcinoma (MCF-7, NCCS, Pune, India) cell line was used for checking the anticancer potential of the rutin loaded and folic acid functionalized CDs.The detailed procedure for the cytotoxicity assay and measurement of intracellular ROS were described in our earlier publications [19]. In brief, cells were first seeded in 96-well plates and exposed to different concentrations (10–50 μg/mL) of CDs, RUT or FA-CDs-RUT for 48 h. After that cell viability was measured via standard MTT assay by taking the absorbance at 570 nm. For measuring intracellular ROS, following treatment with 50 μg/mL of CDs, RUT or FA-CDs-RUT for 48 h cells were incubated with (2, 7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate) H2DCFDA and fluorescence was taken at Ex/Em = 495 nm/525 nm.

The bidirectional wound healing assay of MCF-7 cells was performed according to the method described by Sarkar et al. [19]. At first, MCF-7 cells were cultured and grown in 12 well plates with about 80 % confluency, and then the cell layer was scratched via a pipette tip in order to create a bidirectional wound. After that the cells were treated with CDs, RUT and FA-CDs-RUT for 24 h, and the bright field images were taken under microscope.

Migration assay was done with MCF-7 cells. Cells were collected from a 70–80 % confluent flask and resuspended in serum free media. In a 24 well plate 10 % serum containing DMEM was added (bottom chamber) and cell culture inserts with 8 μM pores (BD Biosciences) were placed. Then the 1 × 105 cells were added into the cell culture inserts (upper chamber). The cells were incubated at 37 °C with 5 % humidity for 24 h. The cells in the upper chamber were washed away with PBS and scraped off gently with cotton buds. The inserts were fixed and stained with a Differential stating kit and the entire membrane was gently cut and mounted in a glass slide. The micrographs were taken at 20× magnification.

MCF-7 cells were first treated with CDs, RUT and FA-CDs-RUT for 48 h, and then lysed with RIPA buffer in presence of protease as well as phosphatase inhibitor cocktail. After that the cellular proteins were collected following centrifugation and quantified by bicinchoninic acid (BCA) assay [19]. Equal amount of proteins from different experimental groups were then resolved by 10–12 % sodium dodecyl sulphate-polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (SDS-PAGE). The proteins were then electrophoretically transferred into polyvinylidene fluoride membrane, blocked with 1 % BSA solution, and incubated with different primary antibodies overnight. After that the membranes were washed and incubated again with horseradish peroxidase-conjugated secondary antibodies. Finally, the protein bands were developed via exposure with ECL (enhanced chemiluminescence) solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Development of in vivo solid tumor model and anticancer activity checking
ce:section.ce:para:
The detailed method for the development of EAC (Ehrlich ascites carcinoma) bearing solid tumors in Swiss albino mice were described in our earlier publication [20]. The solid tumor bearing mice were distributed among four groups, as follows:
Group I- Control/Untreated tumor (EAC)
Group II- CDs treatment group (18.6 mg/kg body weight)
Group III- RUT treatment group (10 mg/kg body weight)
GroupIV- FA-CDs-RUT treatment group (28.6 mg/kg body weight)
The mice were intravenously injected with the formulations on alternate days for 14 days, and tumor mass and volume were measured as described in our previous publication [20].

Animal experiments were done by following the Institutional Animal Ethics Committee, Bose Institute, Kolkata [IAEC/BI/142/2019] rules, and the work was also approved by Committee for the Purpose of Control and Supervision on Experiments on Animals, 1796/PO/Ere/S/14/CPCSEA) [20].

Non-tumorous Swiss albino mice were intravenously injected with CDs, RUT and FA-CDs-RUT on alternate days for 14 days with an equivalent rutin (drug) dose of 10 mg/kg. The serum levels of alanine aminotransferase (ALT), alkaline phosphatase (ALP), creatinine and blood urea nitrogen (BUN) were estimated as instructed in the kits (Span Diagnostics Ltd., India) after the experimental period.

Molecular docking was performed using CB-DOCK2 web server [21]. The server detects the cavities and performs blind docking of ligand with the protein. Bcl-2 (PDB ID: 1GJH) and the ligand (rutin, .mol format) was drawn using a tool provided in the server and uploaded. For docking study five numbers of cavities were chosen by default. The binding pose with highest binding affinity was selected and visualized using BIOVIA Discovery Studio. 2D interaction plots were also generated using Discovery studio.
All the experiments were performed in triplicates and the results are shown as their mean ± SD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro anticancer activity of FA-CDs-RUT nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: A dose-dependent cytotoxic activity of CDs, RUT and FA-CDs-RUT was observed within the concentration range from 10 to 50 μg/mL after 48 h of treatment. FA-CDs-RUT exhibited the highest anticancer activity as compared with bare CDs and RUT (Fig. 3a). Optical images of the cells treated with CDs, RUT and FA-CDs-RUT were also evaluated to confirm their cytotoxic activity (Fig. 3b). The decreased number of cells with obvious cellular damage also indicates the highest cytotoxic index of the nanohybrids, FA-CDs-RUT.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Oxidative stress and induction of apoptosis
ce:section.ce:para: The intracellular ROS levels were measured after treatment with CDs, RUT and FA-CDs-RUT at a concentration of 50 μg/mL. The green fluorescence intensity which is equivalent to the amount of intracellular ROS generation was increased to the highest extent when the cells were exposed to the FA-CDs-RUT nanohybrid ( Fig. 3c). Further we have checked the occurrence of apoptosis in MCF-7 cells. We observed an up-regulated Bax and down-regulated Bcl-2 protein expressions following treatment with FA-CDs-RUT(Fig. 3d). Besides, FA-CDs-RUT also augmented the cytosolic protein expression of Cytochrome c as well as Caspase 9 and Caspase 3 (Fig. 4a), thereby indicating the activation of mitochondrial apoptotic pathway. However, the apoptotic response of the nanohybrid was maximum towards MCF-7 cells as compared with bare CDs and RUT.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro effect of FA-CDs-RUT on retardation of cell migration
In vivo anticancer activity and systemic toxicity assessment of FA-CDs-RUT nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: FA-CDs-RUT nanohybrid treatment with an equivalent rutin/drug dose of 10 mg/kg body weightsignificantly reduced both tumor volume and mass as compared withbare CDs and RUT treatment groups, whereas the tumor size increased constantly for 14 days in the untreated tumor control group (Fig. 6 a-c). Besides, no behavioral abnormality, mortality and significant difference in body weight were observed among the treatment groups (Fig. 6d).

The serum levels of hepatic and renal toxicity markers were also not significantly changed in non-tumorous mice following treatment with the nanoformulations, thereby confirming their non-toxic nature towards vital body organs (Fig. 7 ).

We performed molecular docking study of Bcl-2 (PDB: 1GJH) with rutin (RUT) molecule to identify the nature of binding. To this purpose, we used CB-Dock2 web server [21]. We found that the most probable binding pose of rutin is predicted have binding affinity of −7.6 KCal/mol. It can be seen from the Fig. 8a that the rutin binds to Bcl-2 mainly in hydrophilic region via hydrogen bonding and other types of non-covalent interactions ( Fig. 8b).We believe that the RUTregulates the cell growth by binding to Bcl-2.

In conclusion, a novel rutin loaded and FA tagged CDs (FA-CDs-RUT) have been successfully fabricated as a smart drug delivery nanoplatform for targeted delivery of a natural hydrophobic flavonoid, rutin, into breast cancer cells. Conjugation of FA with the CDs subsequently improved the site-specific delivery of rutin towards folate receptors overexpressed breast cancer cells. In addition, this nanohybrid showed pH-dependent drug release behaviour. Furthermore, the in vitro study demonstrated that the nanohybrid exhibit profound anticancer efficacy by producing intracellular ROS which consequently induced mitochondria-mediated apoptosis. Besides, the in vivo study also showed that the nanohybrid exhibits a significant antitumor effect in tumor-bearing mice and did not impart any significant alterations in blood biochemical parameters. Molecular docking study revealed that rutin can strongly bind to Bcl-2 suggesting the possible regulation mechanism via Bcl-2. Overall, our study reports the development of a potential drug delivery system for tumor therapy.

Neeraj Tejwan: Visualization, Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis, Validation, Writing- Original draft preparation; Anirudh Sharma and Th. Abhishek Singh: Data curation, Formal analysis, Validation; Pritam Sadhukhan: Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis, Validation; Joydeep Das and Parames C. Sil: Supervision, Conceptualization, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Writing- Original draft preparation, Writing- Reviewing and Editing, Funding Acquisition. Madushmita Hatimuria: Formal analysis, Data curation. Ashok Pabbathi: Software,Validation, Formal analysis, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank Shoolini University, Solan, H.P. and Bose institute, Kolkata, W.B. for providing instrumental facilities. A.P. would like to thank DST, INDIA for the research grant in DST-INSPIRE Faculty Scheme (Award number: IFA20-CH-333).
